01 — About

Who I Am

I'm Meet Bhojani, a finance student at Penn State's Smeal College of Business, pursuing a Bachelor of Science in Finance alongside the Smeal Real Estate Analysis & Development Certificate, with coursework spanning Security Analysis, Corporate Finance, and Real Estate Fundamentals.

What sets me apart is a dual track of experience: two summers leading data-driven marketing campaigns and retail expansion at a family-run manufacturing company in Gujarat, India, progressing from intern to Head of Marketing, while simultaneously developing the quantitative discipline of a finance analyst through DCF modeling, credit analysis, and fixed income work.

My goal is to bring both commercial instinct and analytical rigor to a role in investment banking or corporate finance, where decisions are grounded in numbers but shaped by judgment.

06 — Articles & Insights

Thought Leadership

The following articles explore current trends in finance, valuation, and business strategy — written for professionals and students navigating the markets.

Corporate Finance5 min read · By Meet Bhojani

Numbers Don’t Lie — But EPS Does

EPS is one of the most misleading metrics in business finance. Here is why return on invested capital is the number that actually separates companies that create value from those that only appear to.

Photo: @studentofprana via Unsplash

Every quarter, financial media fixates on earnings per share. Analysts repeat it, headlines feature it, and investors trade on it. The problem is that EPS is one of the most misleading metrics in business finance, and most people never stop to question it.

EPS can be engineered through share buybacks, distorted by accounting choices, and inflated through debt-financed acquisitions that quietly destroy long-term value. A company can post rising EPS for years while becoming a weaker business underneath. If you are making decisions based on EPS alone, you are reading the summary of a book someone rewrote to look better.

The Metric That Actually Matters

Return on Invested Capital measures how efficiently a company converts invested capital into after-tax operating profit. That single question tells you more about business quality than any earnings headline.

The key comparison is ROIC versus WACC — the weighted average cost of capital. When ROIC exceeds WACC, value is being created. When it falls below, capital is being destroyed, even if reported earnings are climbing.

Amazon Is the Proof

For much of its history, Amazon reported minimal or negative earnings. By a pure EPS standard, it looked like a struggling company. What was actually happening was the opposite. Amazon was reinvesting aggressively in AWS infrastructure at very high rates of return, building economic value that would eventually dwarf what any short-term earnings figure could show. That is the difference between a business that looks profitable and a business that actually is.

The Reinvestment Rate Equation

Value creation comes down to two variables: ROIC — how efficiently the business earns on what it invests — and the reinvestment rate — how much of those earnings go back into the business. As Aswath Damodaran explains, sustainable growth equals ROIC multiplied by the reinvestment rate. A company with 15% ROIC reinvesting 50% of earnings grows at roughly 7.5%, but only if those reinvestments keep earning that same return.

I applied this framework directly in my own Amazon DCF model, building segment-level ROIC assumptions for AWS and e-commerce separately rather than projecting a single top-line revenue figure. The results told a very different story than the income statement alone would have.

What This Changes for You

Once you understand ROIC, you cannot read financial statements the same way again. You stop treating the income statement as the whole story and start asking how much capital a business consumed to generate a given profit, and whether reinvestment is creating value or quietly eroding it. Those are the questions that separate analysts who add real insight from those who just report what the spreadsheet shows.

Further Reading:Damodaran — Corporate FinanceMcKinsey — ValuationInvestopedia — ROIC
Real Estate Finance5 min read · By Meet Bhojani

Real Estate Is Not Always a Good Investment — Here Is How to Tell the Difference

Most people who own property think like homeowners. Investors ask an entirely different set of questions — about cap rates, leverage, and whether the numbers actually make sense.

Photo: @tierramallorca via Unsplash

Growing up, I always heard people say things like “we bought this as an investment.” It sounded obvious, like owning property automatically meant you were investing. But once I started studying finance at Penn State, I realised that is not really true. Most people who own real estate are not thinking like investors. They are thinking like homeowners. And that difference actually matters more than people think.

Homeowner vs Investor Mindset

A homeowner buys a property for stability, comfort, and maybe the hope that prices go up. There is nothing wrong with that. But that is not really how an investor thinks. An investor looks at the same property and asks very different questions: How much income does this generate? What are the costs? And most importantly — what return am I actually getting for the price I am paying?

That is where the idea of a cap rate comes in. It is simply net operating income divided by the purchase price. If a property makes $80,000 a year and costs $1,000,000, the cap rate is 8%. That number forces you to think logically instead of just relying on the idea that “real estate always goes up.”

Leverage Changes the Game

One thing that makes real estate different is how much you can borrow. You might only put down 25 to 35% and finance the rest. When things go well, this can amplify returns significantly. But leverage works both ways. If income drops or prices fall, that same leverage works against you. This is why debt service coverage ratios and stress testing are so important — before learning this, I never really thought about how much risk sits beneath the surface of a real estate deal.

What I Have Learned at Smeal

Studying real estate at Smeal has changed how I see property completely. Now I think in terms of pro formas, cash flows, and how deals are structured rather than just the property itself. Small assumptions — like rent growth or development timing — can completely change whether something is a good investment or not. Real estate is not automatically a good investment. It only becomes one if the numbers actually make sense.

Further Reading:Investopedia — Cap RatesInvestopedia — DSCRHBR — Real Estate

Game Analytics — Win / Loss / Push Breakdown
♠ Blackjack & Finance
Blackjack is a masterclass in expected value (EV) and decision-making under uncertainty — the same analytical framework that underpins financial modelling. Basic strategy reduces the house edge below 0.5%, mirroring how a disciplined investment process minimises systematic risk. The Kelly Criterion, used by professional card counters for optimal bet sizing, is the identical formula portfolio managers apply for position sizing.
Bankroll Equity Curve
Cumulative P&L over time — mirrors a portfolio NAV chart.
Play games to see your equity curve.
Kelly Criterion — Optimal Bet Size
Based on your win rate, how much of your bankroll per bet?
Kelly f* = (bp – q) / b where b=1.
Expected Value per Hand
Your actual EV vs. the house edge.

Your Edge
EV = (wins × avg_win) – (losses × avg_loss)

Profit Doesn’t Always Mean Value
Strategic Finance · · Meet Bhojani

Image: expensein.com — Basics of Strategic Finance

Every quarter, financial media fixates on earnings per share. Analysts repeat it, headlines feature it, and investors trade on it. The problem is that EPS is one of the most misleading metrics in business finance — and most people never stop to question it.

EPS can be engineered through share buybacks, distorted by accounting choices, and inflated through debt-financed acquisitions that quietly destroy long-term value. A company can post rising EPS for years while becoming a weaker business underneath. If you are making decisions based on EPS alone, you are reading the summary of a book someone rewrote to look better.

What ROIC Actually Measures

Return on Invested Capital tells you how efficiently a company converts its capital into after-tax operating profit. If your ROIC is higher than your cost of capital, you are creating value. If it is lower, you are destroying it — even if your earnings look great on paper.

This is the trap a lot of investors fall into. A company can post rising earnings for years while quietly burning shareholder value if it keeps reinvesting at poor returns.

The Amazon Example Everyone Gets Wrong

For most of its early history, Amazon reported barely any earnings. By EPS logic, it looked like a terrible company. But it was actually one of the greatest value creators of the last 30 years — because it was reinvesting aggressively in AWS at extremely high returns. That is exactly what the ROIC lens reveals. Earnings are backward-looking noise. ROIC shows you what a business is actually doing with its money.

I applied this framework directly in my own Amazon DCF model, building segment-level ROIC assumptions for AWS and e-commerce separately rather than projecting a single top-line figure. The results told a very different story than the income statement alone.

Growth Alone Means Nothing

As Professor Aswath Damodaran explains, sustainable growth equals ROIC multiplied by the reinvestment rate. A company with 15% ROIC reinvesting half its earnings grows at roughly 7.5% — but only if those new investments earn that same 15% return. The moment ROIC drops below the cost of capital, growth stops being an advantage and starts making things worse. More growth just means more value destruction at that point.

How This Changes the Way You Read Financials

Once you start thinking in ROIC terms, you stop reading the income statement alone. You start asking how much capital the company consumed to generate that profit, and whether it is getting better or worse at deploying that capital over time. Those are the questions that separate strong analysts from average ones. Anyone can find the earnings line. Fewer people know how to connect the balance sheet to operating performance and actually ask the right questions about capital efficiency.

The companies that look good on the surface are not always the ones worth owning. ROIC is how you tell the difference.

Further Reading
ExpenseIn — Strategic Finance Damodaran — Corporate Finance Investopedia — ROIC
Amazon (AMZN) — Intrinsic Value Analysis
Independent Research · January 2026 · Meet Bhojani
Background & Objective

I chose Amazon for this project because it is one of the most analytically interesting companies in the market — not because it is easy to value, but because it is genuinely hard. Amazon runs two fundamentally different businesses inside one stock ticker: a high-margin, fast-growing cloud infrastructure platform (AWS) and a lower-margin, capital-intensive global retail operation. Most analysts either collapse these into a single model and lose precision, or they separate them and lose sight of how the cash flows interact. My goal was to build a model that treated each segment on its own terms, then arrive at a consolidated intrinsic value I could actually defend.

Key Assumptions
9.2%
WACC (Base Case)
18%
AWS Revenue CAGR (Yr 1–3)
3.0%
Terminal Growth Rate
11%
E-Commerce CAGR (Yr 1–3)
38%
AWS Operating Margin
5yr
Projection Horizon

The WACC of 9.2% reflects Amazon's strong investment-grade credit profile, a beta of approximately 1.15, and a risk-free rate based on the 10-year Treasury yield at the time of analysis. I ran three scenarios — bear, base, and bull — varying revenue growth and margin assumptions independently for each segment to understand how sensitive the intrinsic value was to each driver.

AWS — The Engine of Value

AWS contributed roughly 17% of Amazon's total revenue in the most recent fiscal year, yet it generates the overwhelming majority of the company's operating income — and nearly all of its economic value creation. When I modelled AWS separately, applying an 18% revenue CAGR for years one through three tapering to 12% in years four and five, and held operating margins steady at 38%, the AWS segment alone accounted for over 70% of my total intrinsic value estimate. That is not a modelling artefact — it reflects the economic reality that AWS is a high-ROIC, capital-light software business embedded inside a capital-heavy retail company.

The key risk to this segment is competitive pressure from Microsoft Azure and Google Cloud, both of which have closed the gap meaningfully over the last few years. I factored this into the bear case by reducing the AWS CAGR to 12% from year one and compressing margins by 3 percentage points — a scenario that shaved roughly 18% off my base case valuation.

E-Commerce & Advertising

Amazon's retail segment is a harder story to tell cleanly. The headline margins are thin — low single digits on product sales — but the business generates substantial free cash flow through working capital dynamics that are easy to miss if you only look at the income statement. Amazon collects cash from customers before it pays suppliers, which means the business self-funds much of its growth through the operating cycle rather than requiring external capital.

I modelled e-commerce at 11% revenue growth in the near term, reflecting continued international expansion and Prime membership penetration, with modest margin improvement as advertising revenue — which carries margins closer to 40% — grows as a proportion of the overall mix. Amazon's advertising business is now a meaningful earnings driver in its own right, and models that treat it as a footnote are underestimating future profitability.

Reinvestment & Capital Allocation

One of the most important — and underappreciated — aspects of Amazon's financial story is how aggressively the company reinvests. For most of its history, Amazon converted nearly all of its operating cash flow back into the business, whether through fulfilment centre build-outs, data centre capacity for AWS, or new business experiments. The market rewarded this with a premium valuation because ROIC on those reinvestments was demonstrably high.

In my model, I used a reinvestment rate of approximately 55% in years one through three, declining gradually as the business matures. The resulting free cash flow profile starts modest and ramps meaningfully in years four and five — which is why Amazon's valuation has always required conviction about the long-term rather than near-term earnings.

Methodology
5-Year DCF Segment Revenue Modeling WACC Sensitivity Analysis Comparable Company Analysis Precedent Transactions Margin Expansion Modeling Terminal Value (Gordon Growth) Bear / Base / Bull Scenarios

"Based on my base case assumptions, I estimated Amazon's intrinsic
